data.title: Sotera Health Non-GAAP EPS of $0.20, revenue of $294M
data.description:
• Sotera Health press release (NASDAQ:SHC): Q2 Non-GAAP EPS of $0.20.
  • Revenue of $294M.
  • Raised both full-year 2025 net revenues outlook to 4.5% - 6.0% and Adjusted EBITDA outlook to 6.0% - 7.5%, on a constant currency basis

data.title: Scripps outlines portfolio optimization and debt reduction strategy amid regulatory shifts
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: The E.W. Scripps Company (SSP) Q2 2025

Management View

  • CEO Adam P. Symson highlighted the completion of the broadcast industry's first station swap transaction, a new multiyear WNBA agreement, and the addition of a fourth NHL team for Scripps Sports, stating, "We delivered a very solid financial performance for the second quarter in the face of a soft advertising climate. That included 900 basis points of Scripps Networks margin improvement." Symson emphasized that these efforts have resulted in a significant reduction of the debt leverage ratio and that "the 2 major refinancing transactions this year have taken care of all of our major short-term maturities."
  • Symson described regulatory changes as a catalyst for growth, saying, "Scripps will be able to go deeper in its local coverage with 3 new duopolies in the Mountain West region. The improved economics will allow us to grow our commitment to quality news gathering and other local programming like sports for the communities we serve."
  • CFO Jason P. Combs reported, "We closed on the placement of $750 million of new senior secured second-lien notes... We have now used the proceeds of this offering to pay off the company's outstanding senior notes that were set to mature in 2027, prepay a portion of our Term Loan B-2 that is due in 2028 and pay off a portion of our revolving credit facilities, leaving $36 million remaining."

Outlook

  • Management expects Q3 Local Media division revenue to be down in the mid- to high 20% range, with core revenue about flat and expenses down in the low to mid-single-digit percent range.
  • For Scripps Networks, revenue is expected to be down low single digits, and expenses down in the mid-single-digit range for Q3.
  • Combs updated full-year guidance, stating, "We now expect our cash interest paid to be between $170 million and $175 million, we expect CapEx to be $45 million to $50 million, and we expect cash taxes paid to be $5 million to $10 million. All of these adjustments will drive incremental cash flow this year."

Financial Results

  • Scripps Networks revenue for Q2 was $206 million. Segment profit was $56 million, with a segment margin of 27%, up from 18% in Q2 2024.
  • Local Media segment profit was nearly $56 million, compared to $88 million in Q2 2024.
  • CTV revenue for the quarter was up 57%.
  • Second quarter EPS was a loss of ($0.59). The quarter included $38 million of financing transaction costs, a $31 million gain on the sale of the West Palm Beach station building, a $5.6 million write-off of deferred financing costs, and a $3 million loss on extinguishment of debt. These items increased the loss by $0.13 per share. The preferred stock dividend reduced EPS by $0.18 this quarter.
  • Cash and cash equivalents at June 30 totaled $31.7 million. Net leverage at the end of Q2 was 4.4x, a half turn below the end of Q1.

Q&A

  • Daniel Louis Kurnos, The Benchmark Company: Asked about the trajectory and targets for further portfolio optimization amid deregulation. Symson responded that the company is "going to do everything in our power to take advantage of this moment," with swaps and select asset sales targeted for portfolio improvement and accelerated debt paydown.
  • Kurnos followed up on Q3 guidance, to which Combs clarified, "political in sort of this odd year is going to be similar to past odd years, kind of mid $20-ish million. It will build throughout the year with the majority of it hitting in Q4."
  • Michael A. Kupinski, NOBLE Capital Markets: Probed the core advertising environment and advertiser sentiment. Combs stated, "We came in down 2% versus the prior year, that's sort of best-in-class from what I've seen across the industry and certainly as it ties directly back to our sports strategy."
  • Kupinski inquired about the impact of declining search traffic. Symson noted, "We do employ a number of different technical solutions to retain the value of our IP and block chatbots from scraping our websites... I don't necessarily think that the integration of AI into core search is going to be a net positive for publishers."
  • Craig Anthony Huber, Huber Research Partners, asked about CBS nonrenewal events and retrans sub losses. Symson described the CBS Atlanta change as "more of a one-off" and reiterated expectations for net retrans margin expansion. Combs added, "We continue to see sort of a down mid-single-digit 12-month trend on our sub churn."
  • Steven Lee Cahall, Wells Fargo, asked about structural ad pressures. Symson said, "The first one is our sports strategy... It's allowing us both through upfront and scatter to ensure we're not just selling that time period but we're selling across network, we're selling CTV."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts demonstrated a cautious but constructive tone, probing for more clarity on guidance, advertiser sentiment, and regulatory timing. Questions reflected concerns about the pace of regulatory change, advertising headwinds, and the durability of sports-driven results.
  • Management maintained a confident stance in prepared remarks but acknowledged macroeconomic uncertainty, with Symson stating, "uncertainty is nobody's friend," and Combs noting "hesitancy is the right word to throw out there for advertisers." The tone was slightly more defensive in the Q&A compared to prepared remarks as they addressed persistent questions on advertising and regulatory timing.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s confidence remained steady on portfolio optimization and debt reduction, but there was increased focus on navigating a soft advertising environment and macroeconomic unpredictability. Analyst tone was more pressing on near-term visibility and the sustainability of sports-driven growth.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Management introduced a new station swap transaction and reported further progress in refinancing and debt retirement, compared to Q1’s focus on refinancing completions and margin expansion.
  • Q2 guidance for Local Media revenue was more negative, with sharper declines expected compared to Q1’s high single-digit down outlook. Networks revenue turned from a flat guide in Q1 to a low single-digit decline for Q3.
  • Scripps Networks segment margin improved from 18% in Q2 2024 to 27% in Q2 2025, while management highlighted a 900 basis point lift.
  • Analyst questions shifted from focus on refinancing and sports asset expansion in Q1 to deeper concern about ad market weakness, regulatory developments, and portfolio optimization in Q2.
  • Management’s tone was slightly more measured regarding advertising recovery, compared to the optimism in Q1 around sports-driven outperformance.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited persistent macroeconomic uncertainty, advertising market hesitancy, and automotive category weakness as key challenges.
  • Regulatory outcomes remain pending, particularly regarding FCC ownership rules and national cap changes, with Symson stating, "it's just going to take a little bit of time."
  • Analyst questions identified the risk of further declines in core advertising and the potential impact of declining search referrals and AI on digital monetization.
  • Sub churn in retransmissions continues at a mid-single-digit rate.

Final Takeaway

The E.W. Scripps Company emphasized a disciplined approach to portfolio optimization and debt reduction, leveraging regulatory changes and its sports strategy to navigate a challenging advertising environment. Management signaled confidence in improving cash flow, margin expansion in networks, and further debt paydown, while acknowledging ongoing macroeconomic uncertainty and a cautious advertising market. The company remains focused on seizing opportunities presented by regulatory shifts to strengthen its local and national footprint and drive value for shareholders.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Microchip dips on Q2 results as analysts highlight improving margins
data.description:
Microchip Technology (NASDAQ:MCHP) was down 4% during Friday market action following second quarter results that surpassed estimates and demonstrated improving gross margins.

Despite the share drop, analysts were impressed with the results, outlook and improving margins.

"Yesterday, Microchip posted good results with guidance above seasonality, but we believe it was below elevated expectations, which caused the stock to sell off," said Citi analyst Christopher Danely, in an investor note. "We believe all the upturn levers are still there for MCHP with 80% incremental gross margins and some of the highest revenue growth among peers, and we continue to expect MCHP to exhibit the highest EPS growth."

Citi reiterated its Buy rating and $90 price target on the Arizona-based semiconductor firm.

KeyBanc maintained its Overweight rating and $85 price target following the results.

"Mgmt expressed confidence in continued above seasonal revenue growth and improving cycle dynamics, highlighting: 1) decreasing inventory at disty and direct customers; 2) the sell-in/sell-through gap decreased from $103M last qtr to $49M in F1Q; and 3) backlog for F2Q is meaningfully higher than F1Q," said KeyBanc analysts John Vinh and Ryan Rosumny, in an investor note.

Meanwhile, Stifel maintained its Buy rating and $82 price target. Stifel also increased its full-year 2026 earnings per share estimate to $1.47 from $1.35 and increased its 2026 revenue estimate to $4.6B from $4.56B.

"Overall, we were impressed with MCHP's Jun Q print and Sep Q guide, and accordingly we are raising our estimates to reflect the positive dynamics discussed on the call," said Stifel analysts, led by Tore Svanberg, in a note. "While tariff uncertainty continues to weigh on companies across our coverage universe in the NT, we remind investors that MCHP has already progressed through a significant NT correction (revenues declined -44.3% y/y in CY24A) and, even with our raised estimates, we are modeling CY25E to decline -8.8% y/y, which we believe appropriately balances these risks with the company-specific momentum arising from MCHP's Nine Point Recovery Plan."

Microchip's peers such as Texas Instruments (TXN) and STMicroelectronics (STM) were up 0.5% and 2%, respectively.

data.title: Plug Power Q2 results on deck: Here's what to expect
data.description:
Analysts see Plug Power’s (NASDAQ:PLUG) second-quarter results, which is scheduled for August 11, after market close, posting a solid year-on-year jump.

While the company is expected to continue incurring losses, PLUG may trim it down by over 58% compared to last year to -$0.15. Revenue is likely to come in at $157.97M, translating to a 10.2% jump compared to the corresponding quarter last year.

This quarter, investors are likely to focus on how the company’s growth initiatives and strategic partnerships are contributing to revenue and profitability.

Last month, Plug Power announced a new multi-year enhanced supply agreement with "a leading U.S.-based industrial gas company and longtime hydrogen partner. In June, the company expanded its partnership with Allied Green Ammonia with a new 2-electrolyzer opportunity linked to a sustainable fuels project in Uzbekistan.

In Q1, the company posted mixed results with revenue beating estimates but EPS lagging behind. The company maintained its Q2 revenue outlook in the range of $140 million to $180 million. Additionally, the company anticipates further improvement in gross margin and working capital performance from Q1 2025 onward, continuing through the rest of the year.

The management maintained the target of becoming gross margin break-even by the end of the year.

Over the last 3 months, PLUG’s EPS estimates have seen 4 upward revisions and 2 downward moves, while revenue estimates have seen 8 upward revisions and 9 downward moves.

data.title: Arcosa outlines $2.9B revenue and 30% EBITDA growth targets for 2025 as strategic initiatives drive record results
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Arcosa, Inc. (ACA) Q2 2025

Management View

  • Antonio Carrillo, President and CEO, reported "a record quarter that reflects the positive momentum for our strategic initiatives," highlighting Arcosa's deliberate actions to strengthen growth businesses, streamline the portfolio, reduce cyclicality, and expand margins. He stated that the successful integration of Stavola, acquired in October 2024, significantly contributed to growth, increasing consolidated revenues by 14% and expanding adjusted EBITDA margin by 250 basis points. "Contribution from Stavola more than compensated for weather-related challenges in our organic legacy Construction Products business, which experienced above-average rainfall throughout the quarter," Carrillo said.
  • Carrillo emphasized strong pricing gains in Aggregates, a record quarter for Engineered Structures, and positive performance in the barge business. He noted that Arcosa delivered strong cash generation and remains on track to achieve its target leverage range of 2 to 2.5x within the next three quarters.
  • Gail M. Peck, CFO, stated, "Second quarter revenues increased 28%, and adjusted segment EBITDA increased 44%, driven by the accretive contribution from Stavola. Margin expanded 310 basis points to 28.3%." Peck further detailed that Stavola delivered a 39% adjusted EBITDA margin for the quarter and that organic pricing was up mid-single digits. "With more normalized weather patterns in July, we experienced strong double-digit volume growth in our Aggregates business supported by Stavola and solid organic growth, which underpins our confidence in the second half of the year," Peck said.

Outlook

  • Arcosa is tightening its full-year 2025 guidance for revenues and adjusted EBITDA while maintaining the midpoint. Carrillo said, "At the midpoint of our range, we anticipate revenues of $2.9 billion, up 17% year-to-year and adjusted EBITDA of $570 million, up 30%, excluding steel components from 2024 results."
  • The company increased its full-year pricing outlook for Aggregates to high single-digit appreciation and expects double-digit volume growth. Carrillo described the company as well positioned for consistent long-term growth, with strong demand in growth businesses and solid backlog visibility in cyclical businesses.
  • The transition of an idle Illinois facility from Wind Towers to Utility Structures is underway, expected to be operational in the second half of 2026, reflecting rising demand for transmission structures.

Financial Results

  • Revenue increased 18% and adjusted EBITDA grew 42% year-over-year, excluding the divested steel components business. Arcosa reported a record adjusted EBITDA margin of 20.9%, up 360 basis points.
  • Aggregates business freight-adjusted revenues rose 15%, with a 21% increase in adjusted cash gross profit. Total volumes increased 6%, while freight-adjusted average sales price per ton rose 8% to $17.83. Adjusted cash gross profit per ton grew 15% to $8.24.
  • Engineered Structures segment revenue increased 7% due to higher Wind Tower volumes. Adjusted segment EBITDA increased 31% and margin reached a record 18.7%. Utility and Related Structures backlog ended at $450 million, up 9% from the start of the year. Wind Towers backlog was nearly $600 million.
  • Transportation Products revenues were up 18%, and adjusted segment EBITDA increased 10%. Barge orders totaled $33 million, with backlog at $277 million. Operating cash flow was $61 million, capex was $28 million, and free cash flow was $39 million.

Q&A

  • Ethan Roberts, Stephens: Asked about the updated guide range and changes at the segment level. Peck responded that "we've maintained the midpoint, as you know, and tightened the range," noting 12% organic growth is expected, with second-half construction projected for high single-digit organic growth.
  • Roberts also inquired on Aggregates' ASP guidance drivers. Peck said, "we had 8% growth in the quarter, total ASP growth. That brings year-to-date growth to about a 10% increase year-over-year," attributing the raise to broad-based pricing gains and successful mid-year increases.
  • Isaac Arthur Sellhausen, Oppenheimer: Asked about Wind Tower capacity. Carrillo explained, "we have 4 plants that could produce wind towers. One...we are transitioning to a transmission tower...our Wind Tower business is also performing very well...The 3 plants that are left for wind are operating...about 60% capacity, probably in New Mexico a little higher."
  • Garik Simha Shmois, Loop Capital: Asked about Aggregates' gross profit per ton and long-term targets. Peck replied they intend to "maintain that growth in GP per ton" through pricing and cost discipline, but have not set an absolute target.
  • Shmois also questioned leverage and acquisition pipeline. Carrillo said they have "a very solid pipeline of bolt-on acquisitions," and as leverage decreases, capital deployment for organic and inorganic growth will be considered.
  • Julio Alberto Romero, Sidoti & Company: Inquired on wind tower policy and margin implications. Carrillo said, "what the industry needs is clarity...the industry is not going anywhere...we have a solid couple of years coming."
  • Romero followed up on customer-driven delays. Carrillo confirmed no delays from funding, only weather-related challenges.
  • Romero also asked about multifamily demand in Aggregates. Carrillo highlighted New Jersey and Texas as strong regions.
  • Brent Edward Thielman, D.A. Davidson: Asked about backlog visibility and wind revenue run rate into 2026. Carrillo said, "we have 3 plants operating...one has orders until 2028, the other two...until the end of '25...we're confident we're going to get there."
  • Thielman asked about the decision to add Utility Structures capacity. Carrillo cited increasing utility CapEx forecasts and a shift to larger poles as reasons for converting the wind plant.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts posed questions on guidance reliability, pricing momentum, and backlog sustainability, displaying a neutral to slightly positive tone as they sought clarification on volume growth and policy clarity.
  • Management maintained a confident and optimistic tone in both prepared remarks and Q&A, especially regarding guidance, demand visibility, and growth prospects. Carrillo repeatedly emphasized confidence and momentum.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analyst tone remains consistent, with a focus on guidance details and operational execution. Management exhibited increased confidence, citing improved visibility and backlog clarity.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for revenues and adjusted EBITDA is unchanged at the midpoint, but the range has tightened, reflecting increased confidence in full-year performance.
  • Strategic focus has shifted to increased transparency in growth businesses, expanded Aggregates disclosures, and accelerated transformation of idle assets into high-demand segments.
  • Key metrics show accelerated revenue, margin, and cash flow growth compared to Q1, with significant contributions from the Stavola acquisition and improved volumes in Aggregates.
  • Analysts continued to probe guidance assumptions, volume growth, and policy impacts; management's tone has become more assertive, highlighting execution and pipeline strength.

Risks and Concerns

  • Weather-related challenges were cited as a significant headwind for organic Construction Products volumes, though offset by acquisitions.
  • Management emphasized the need for policy clarity, particularly in the Wind Tower segment, but expressed relief at recent legislative developments.
  • No material customer-driven delays were reported; funding or DoT delays were not seen as impactful compared to weather.
  • Leverage remains a focus, with ongoing deleveraging efforts after the Stavola acquisition.

Final Takeaway

Arcosa management underlined a record-setting quarter driven by strategic portfolio transformation, strong pricing, and successful integration of the Stavola acquisition. With tightened full-year guidance and a $2.9 billion revenue and $570 million adjusted EBITDA target, the company projects double-digit growth supported by robust backlogs and expanding demand in key end markets. Management expressed confidence in sustaining momentum through disciplined capital allocation, further M&A opportunities, and operational execution, while monitoring weather and policy-related risks.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: AMC Networks raises 2025 free cash flow outlook to $250M as streaming revenue accelerates
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: AMC Networks (AMCX) Q2 2025

Management View

  • Kristin Aigner Dolan, CEO, stated AMC Networks is executing on its strategic plan focused on programming, partnerships, and profitability, emphasizing progress in streaming revenue growth, strong licensing performance, and $96 million in free cash flow for the quarter. She announced, "we are raising our free cash flow outlook to approximately $250 million for the full year." Dolan also highlighted completion of important financing transactions, expecting that "the retirement of our debt at a significant discount will create meaningful shareholder value."
  • CEO Dolan discussed the company's strategy of building fan communities across linear networks, streaming franchises, and the film business, and noted record engagement at San Diego Comic-Con for The Walking Dead, Anne Rice universes, and Shudder.
  • Dolan described AMC’s unique, genre-focused streaming approach, with targeted services like Shudder and Acorn TV fostering high engagement and loyalty. She shared that all streaming services remain priced below $10 per month and that "we implemented rate events at both Acorn and Shudder and still saw year-over-year and sequential improvements in both our rate of churn and engagement across our streaming portfolio."
  • The company is expanding its channel presence, operating more than 20 domestic FAST channels, and is growing internationally with new launches in the U.K., Central and Northern Europe, Iberia, and Latin America. Dolan cited a "multiyear high in subscriber acquisition" for Acorn TV and record box office for Shudder’s Clown in a Cornfield.
  • Dolan noted strong growth for HIDIVE in the anime genre, and highlighted a 25%-plus increase in digital ad commitments in the current upfront, while overall ad volume remains on track with the prior year. AMC is also in advanced talks with Netflix to expand the branded AMC collection content relationship.
  • She announced a new partnership with Runway to leverage generative AI in marketing and programming development, with more details expected in coming months.
  • Patrick O’Connell, CFO, stated, "We are pleased to report another quarter of healthy free cash flow generation with second quarter free cash flow totaling $96 million." He added, "we've increased our outlook and now anticipate approximately $250 million of free cash flow for 2025."

Outlook

  • O’Connell reiterated the updated annual free cash flow outlook of approximately $250 million, up from prior guidance, supported by strong year-to-date performance, programming efficiencies, and cash tax savings due to full interest deductibility. He reaffirmed expectations for consolidated revenue of approximately $2.3 billion and consolidated AOI in the $400 million to $420 million range.
  • Management expects quarterly streaming revenue growth to accelerate as rate events take full effect, projecting full-year streaming revenue growth in the low to mid-teens. Content licensing revenue is anticipated to total approximately $250 million for the year. O’Connell noted, "the third quarter will represent the lowest licensing revenue quarter for the year, and that revenue will pick back up in the fourth quarter."
  • The company expects the fourth quarter to reflect all 2025 streaming price increases, leading to AOI consistent with Q2.

Financial Results

  • Consolidated net revenue for Q2 was $600 million, down 4% year-over-year. Consolidated AOI was $109 million with an 18% margin. Adjusted EPS was $0.69 per share.
  • Domestic Operations revenue was $527 million, with subscription revenue down 1% (despite a 12% decline in affiliate revenue, streaming revenue grew 12%). Streaming subscribers reached 10.4 million. Advertising revenue decreased 18%, attributed to linear ratings declines and lower marketplace pricing.
  • Content licensing revenue for the quarter was $84 million, reflecting strong demand, a music catalog sale, and producer fees related to Apple TV+’s Silo.
  • International segment revenue was $76 million. Subscription revenue (excluding FX) declined 9%, impacted by the Movistar nonrenewal in Spain. Advertising revenue (excluding adjustments and FX) increased 2%.
  • The company reduced net debt to approximately $1.3 billion and improved its consolidated net leverage ratio to 2.7x. $875 million in total liquidity was reported, including $700 million in pro forma cash.
  • AMC repurchased 1.6 million Class A shares for $10 million in Q2, with $125 million remaining on the current authorization.

Q&A

  • Thomas L. Yeh, Morgan Stanley, asked about drivers of the free cash flow upside and trends across revenue buckets. O’Connell attributed the increase primarily to cash tax savings and modest programming savings, clarifying that "cash taxes is the largest component of guidance increase." On revenue trends, he stated that content licensing remains strong, advertising is weaker, and affiliate revenue is stable.
  • Yeh inquired about the Runway partnership. Dolan described Runway as "a tool that allows us to ideate" and not an IP integration, while Dan McDermott noted, "we are an early adopter in the fast-moving space of deploying AI in the service of generative visualization," using AI to cut costs and enhance creative work.
  • John Hodulik, UBS, asked about sustainable streaming subscription growth and ad trends. O’Connell answered, "we feel really good about the acceleration on the streaming side, both in terms of price and units," with pricing power and strong subscriber uptake. Kim Kelleher highlighted a 25%+ increase in digital upfront commitments and ongoing innovation in addressable advertising.
  • Charles Wilber, Guggenheim Securities, questioned areas of advertising success and FAST expansion. Kelleher pointed to strength in QSR, financial, food, and retail categories, while automotive remained weak. Dolan described the international rollout of FAST channels and the technical ease of their expansion.
  • David Joyce, Seaport Research Partners, asked about the impact of distributor renewals on streaming and advertising. Dolan reported strong activation and engagement from Charter and FILO subscribers, with advertising revenue from these bundled deals expected to accrue over time.
  • Steven Cahall, Wells Fargo, asked about the company’s approach to asset composition and capital allocation. O’Connell maintained that AMC’s studio, streaming, and IP portfolio differentiate it, and buybacks remain lower priority to content investment and debt reduction.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was largely inquisitive and focused on sources of cash flow improvement, sustainability of streaming growth, advertising trends, and the strategic use of AI, with some skepticism around advertising headwinds and the impact of distribution changes.
  • Management’s prepared remarks were confident, emphasizing strategic execution and positive momentum. During Q&A, management remained steady, providing detailed clarifications and showing confidence in controllable factors. Phrases like "we feel really good" and "we are pleased" underscored this outlook.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s tone was similarly confident but more assertive about raising the free cash flow outlook and the effectiveness of strategic initiatives. Analyst sentiment remained neutral to slightly positive, with ongoing questions about ad market challenges and sustainability of streaming gains.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The most significant change is the increase in free cash flow outlook from $220 million to $250 million, attributed to tax savings and programming efficiency. Streaming revenue growth accelerated to 12%, up from 8% in Q1, and subscriber numbers increased to 10.4 million from 10.2 million.
  • Content licensing revenue surged to $84 million from $54 million in Q1. Advertising revenue decline deepened to 18% from 15% in Q1, reflecting persistent linear headwinds.
  • Management’s tone shifted from cautious optimism to increased confidence, especially regarding streaming and free cash flow. Analyst focus remained on cash flow drivers, advertising weakness, and the impact of distributor agreements. Strategic priorities remained consistent, but the company signaled more aggressive international FAST expansion and AI adoption.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited continued linear revenue headwinds and a challenging ad market, with lower digital CPMs and ratings declines impacting advertising revenue.
  • International risks remain around subscription revenue, particularly following the Movistar nonrenewal in Spain.
  • Content licensing revenue is subject to timing variability, and management cautioned that Q3 will be the lowest quarter for licensing revenue.
  • Analyst questions highlighted ongoing concerns about advertising softness, the sustainability of streaming growth, and the long-term impact of distribution changes.

Final Takeaway

AMC Networks’ management emphasized strategic execution around programming, partnerships, and profitability, citing accelerating streaming revenue, strong licensing, and higher-than-expected free cash flow generation. The company raised its full-year free cash flow outlook to $250 million and reported increasing digital advertising commitments, international FAST expansion, and early successes in leveraging AI for content creation. Management conveyed continued confidence in navigating industry headwinds while focusing on building fan-driven franchises and prudent capital allocation.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Tempus AI raises 2025 revenue guidance to $1.26B with 89.6% Q2 growth driven by genomics and data deals
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Tempus AI, Inc. (TEM) Q2 2025

Management View

  • Eric P. Lefkofsky, CEO, opened by stating, "Q2 was a fantastic quarter. As I mentioned in my letter, the company is hitting its stride as we approach our tenth anniversary, which is great." He reported revenue increased 89.6% to $314.6 million. Genomics revenue grew 115% to $241.8 million, with oncology volume growth accelerating from 20% last quarter to 26% this quarter, and hereditary testing at 32%.
  • Lefkofsky highlighted, "Data and Services revenue increased 35.7% to 70 -- roughly $73 million, led by Insights, which is our data licensing, which grew almost 41%." Quarterly gross profit rose to $195 million, a roughly 160% increase, with sequential improvement in adjusted EBITDA from about negative $16 million last quarter to negative $5.6 million, approaching breakeven.
  • He announced increased full-year 2025 revenue guidance to $1.26 billion, maintaining adjusted EBITDA forecast of about $5 million, and described a strengthened balance sheet through issuance of $750 million of 0.75% convertible notes and cash and marketable securities at about $290 million.
  • James Rogers, CFO, stated, "On ASPs, as we mentioned in the letter, we did see an increase in the volume of xT CDx. It was about 20% of our xT volume in Q1 grew about 28% in Q2."

Outlook

  • Management increased full-year 2025 revenue guidance to $1.26 billion and maintained the adjusted EBITDA forecast at about $5 million, which Lefkofsky described as "roughly $110 million improvement over last year."
  • Rogers explained that incremental gains in average selling prices (ASPs) are expected over the balance of the year as migration to higher-reimbursed assays continues, targeting 40% of xT volume in CDx by year-end, but not expecting a significant step-up as seen in Q1.

Financial Results

  • Quarterly revenue was reported at $314.6 million, with genomics contributing $241.8 million and hereditary testing at a 32% growth rate. Oncology volume grew to 26% and gross profit reached $195 million. Adjusted EBITDA improved significantly to negative $5.6 million, indicating progress toward breakeven.
  • The balance sheet was strengthened by issuing $750 million in convertible notes and finishing the quarter with about $290 million in cash and marketable securities. Lefkofsky noted "we added about $375 million additional leaves our balance sheet in a really good spot as we approach Q3."

Q&A

  • Daniel Brennan, TD Cowen, asked about the source of strong core genomic volumes and hereditary testing. Lefkofsky explained, "significant sequential volume growth across our entire oncology testing compendium... For us, it's a function of a lot of the things we had put in place in terms of sales force efficiency, realigning some of the territories, improvements we have made across our technology stack."
  • Yuko Oku, Morgan Stanley, questioned ASP trends and new markers. Rogers responded, "we would think over the balance of the year as we kind of approach 40%, you would see kind of incremental gains to overall ASPs...we are still gating that volume."
  • Rachel Vatnsdal Olson, JPMorgan, asked about insights and trial bookings and the AZ Pathos deal. Lefkofsky replied, "bookings, obviously, were super strong in the quarter because the AstraZeneca Pathos deal was actually an April event... we're ahead of where we want it to be."
  • Michael Ryskin, BofA Securities, inquired about pharma data partnerships and investment priorities. Lefkofsky said, "we have a really healthy, robust pipeline of very big deals that we expect to close over some horizon of time... we're making lots of investments in growth."
  • Mark Massaro, BTIG, probed the liquid biopsy business and Ambry. Lefkofsky stated, "our growth rate in liquid has been dramatically faster than the rest of the market because we're at real scale... the core of the growth feels durable and sustainable."
  • Andrew Brackmann, William Blair, asked about acquisitions. Lefkofsky said, "we have historically been opportunistic but not overly acquisitive... we continue to be measured and disciplined."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts showed a positive but probing tone, with Brennan, Vatnsdal Olson, and Massaro seeking color on the durability and breakdown of growth, and the sustainability of the hereditary and data businesses.
  • Management's tone was confident and upbeat during prepared remarks, with Lefkofsky repeatedly emphasizing strong performance, operational discipline, and positive long-term prospects. In Q&A, management maintained confidence but was measured in promising future growth, noting they "try to be conservative and until we see these things play out."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management maintained a positive outlook, but current quarter calls featured more specifics on volume acceleration, operational execution, and strategic deals. There was continued emphasis on discipline and sustainability of growth.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Revenue growth accelerated from 75.4% in Q1 to 89.6% in Q2, with genomics revenue growth increasing from 89% to 115% and oncology volume rising from 20% to 26%.
  • The previous quarter's focus on the AZ-Pathos deal materialized into strong bookings in Q2. Management raised full-year revenue guidance again, reflecting increased confidence. Gross profit saw further gains, and adjusted EBITDA improved sequentially.
  • Guidance language evolved to reinforce operational discipline while also reflecting greater confidence in both the hereditary and data businesses.
  • Analysts in both quarters focused on volume growth, durability, and data deals, but Q2 saw more questions on execution, market share, and the impact of large deals.
  • Management's confidence increased, supported by evidence of execution on sales and technology initiatives.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management identified reimbursement as a gating factor for scaling MRD and other new tests, with Rogers noting, "we are still gating that volume... we're not anticipating xM kind of creating -- offsetting all of the gains that we have."
  • Lefkofsky highlighted the lack of a mechanism in the U.S. health care system to reimburse for AI or algorithms, which limits the financial impact of these products until reimbursement pathways are established.
  • Competitive risks in genomics were acknowledged, particularly with the exit or contraction of other players, but management described Tempus as capturing market share.
  • Analysts raised concerns about the scalability of new initiatives, the sustainability of current growth rates, and potential impacts of funding or reimbursement delays.

Final Takeaway

Tempus AI reported another quarter of rapid growth, highlighted by a major increase in genomics volumes, strong hereditary testing performance, and a significant data licensing deal with AstraZeneca and Pathos. The company raised full-year revenue guidance to $1.26 billion, maintained disciplined cost management, and strengthened its balance sheet, all while approaching adjusted EBITDA breakeven. Management conveyed confidence in the durability of growth and the ability to capture further market share, while continuing to invest in both core diagnostics and new AI-driven data products. Reimbursement and scaling of new products remain key watch points for the remainder of the year.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: AMC Q2 earnings on deck: What to expect
data.description:
AMC Entertainment Holdings (NYSE:AMC) is expected to post second quarter results on Monday, after markets close.

Wall Street expects the Leawood, Kansas-based theater chain to post EPS of -$0.08 on revenue of $1.34 billion, implying a rise of over 30% during the quarter.

AMC, in May, reported a fall in its quarterly revenue as few people flocked to the movie theaters due to the absence of major blockbuster releases and customers keeping a check on their discretionary spending amid macroeconomic uncertainty.

However, analysts are now positive on a recovery, thanks to a consistent release slate over the next several quarters. Industry ticket sales in the U.S. soared 37%, according to data from Box Office Mojo, fueled by the success of movies including A Minecraft Movie, Thunderbolts, Sinners and Disney’s Lilo & Stitch.

"With box office expected to be more consistent in the coming quarters, we expect AMC’s EBITDA to cover interest expense, relieving its need to issue more shares," noted Wedbush analyst Alicia Reese adding that AMC is positioned to gain market share in 2025 and 2026 in North America and the UK/EU.

Over the last two years, AMC has beaten EPS estimates 63% of the time and has beaten revenue estimates 75% of the time.

Seeking Alpha analysts have rated the stock a Sell, while Wall Street and Seeking Alpha’s Quant rating consider it a Hold.

Over the last three months, EPS estimates have seen two upward revisions, compared to one downward revisions. Revenue estimates have seen four upward revisions versus two downward moves.

The stock has lost over 28% so far this year, compared to the near 8% rise in the broader S&P 500 Index.

data.title: Trump administration reportedly seeks higher ex-US drug prices in pursuit of MFN policy
data.description:
The U.S. government has been discussing with drugmakers how to raise overseas drug prices as President Donald Trump seeks to implement his Most Favored Nation policy, which attempts to lower U.S. drug prices to the level of other wealthy nations, Reuters reported.

Citing a White House official, the news agency reported that the administration even offered to support drugmakers in their negotiations with overseas governments if they follow the MFN policy, which Trump reiterated in letters sent to 17 leading pharma executives last week.

The CEOs of major U.S. pharmaceutical companies Gilead (GILD), Bristol Myers (BMY), J&J (NYSE:JNJ), Regeneron (REGN), Amgen (AMGN), and AbbVie (ABBV) received Trump’s letters along with Lilly (NYSE:LLY), Pfizer (NYSE:PFE), and Merck (NYSE:MRK).

The letters also targeted European drugmakers, including Sanofi (SNY), GSK (GSK), AstraZeneca (AZN), Novo Nordisk (NVO), Roche (OTCQX:RHHBY), and Novartis (NVS).

Two sources said that the Trump Administration has sought ideas from some pharma companies on how to raise overseas drug prices, as multiple meetings took place over the last several months aiming to address the pricing issue without a concurrent decrease in R&D spending.

The White House official added that both parties requested each other’s ideas during the interactions, which were described as collaborative.

Plans to increase drug prices in Europe have taken center stage during the Trump administration’s talks with the industry, a senior European pharma executive said, adding that it has already discussed the issue with the region’s governments.

A second executive noted that White House officials have continuously met his company representatives and discussed ways to raise overseas drug prices.

"There's a big push from the administration to drive up prices outside the U.S.," added the executive, who was not authorized to comment about the matter.

data.title: Rolls-Royce nears deal to offload $5.4B U.K. pension pot - Bloomberg
data.description:
Rolls-Royce (OTCPK:RYCEY) (OTCPK:RYCEF) is close to announcing a deal with pension buyout firm Pension Insurance Corp. to offload nearly £4B (~$5.4B) in pension liabilities off its balance sheet, Bloomberg reported Friday.

A buyout of Rolls-Royce's (OTCPK:RYCEY) (OTCPK:RYCEF) pension would be the largest U.K. risk-transfer so far this year, according to the report, which said the U.K. pension buyout market - where companies pay insurers to take on their financial obligations to pensioners - has been booming in recent years as higher interest rates reduced the value of plans' liabilities.

The company, which is best known for the aircraft engines it makes for Airbus and Boeing, has overhauled its retirement fund over the years after being forced in the past two decades to plug shortfalls that weighed on its earnings performance.

Rolls-Royce (OTCPK:RYCEY) (OTCPK:RYCEF) shares, which have gained 90% YTD after almost doubling in 2024, are little changed Friday in London trading.
